title: Rock cakes
subtitle: Fruited drop scones with a rough, craggy surface
excerpt: Rock cakes are dropped onto the tray in rough mounds rather than cut into neat shapes. The craggy surface and domed top are the point — they should look like rocks. The texture is between a scone and a cake, with a crisp outside and a slightly doughy centre.
sourceNotes: Rock cakes are a British home-baking staple documented from the Victorian period. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136 — rock cakes appear in the cakes chapter as a simple fruit bake requiring no rolling or shaping.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked and cooled. Warm at 180°C for 8 minutes from frozen.
recipe.batchNotes: Best on the day or the day after. Keep in an airtight tin for up to 3 days.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before and warm briefly before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique of placing unbaked dough or batter onto a tray using two spoons rather than rolling and cutting. The rough shape produced by dropping is intentional — a craggy, irregular mound bakes into a product with varied texture: crisp points and edges from the exposed surface, a softer centre underneath. Rock cakes, Welsh cakes, and some cookies use this approach.

content.text: : two spoons or a dessertspoon and your fingers place rough mounds on the tray. Don't smooth them — the irregular surface is what gives rock cakes their texture. Leave space for spread, but they don't spread much.

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Rub {{unsalted-butter}} into {{self-raising-flour}} until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs. Stir in {{caster-sugar}} and {{mixed-spice}}, then fold through {{currants}} and {{mixed-peel}} if using. Add the beaten {{eggs}} and {{whole-milk}} and mix to a stiff, rough mixture — it should hold its shape when heaped but not be smooth. Spoon 12 rough mounds onto lined baking trays, leaving 4 cm between them. Bake for 12–15 minutes until the peaks are golden and the edges are set. Cool on a wire rack.
